item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is Public Domain.

item.title: i know its been -quite- some time since this was written, but...
item.content:encoded:
something i would find fascinating would be some form of followup- regardless of the information/trust issues-
i genuinely think there would have been a non-zero chance of the GOC taking a potshot anyway if they knew it was a Foundation craft-

in multiple instances/realities, their operatives have caused issues/gotten themselves and others killed due to their trigger-happy,sometimes borderline idiotically gung-ho nature- the anomalous Fishing boats incident that killed literally everyone involved, for example, or that old one involving the Teleporting chair and a wood-chipper that, again, killed the entire team AND changed a harmless item to an actively hostile and lethal one
- the tragedy here may have been born of miscommunication, but given their normally mutually irrevocably incompatible organizational goals/methods past "protect humanity", i …genuinely think the Foundation was justified in not communicating….
To me the GOC often feels like a group of panicked children with weapons,wildly lashing out at things they fear/dont understand- sometimes they eliminate a genuine threat, sometimes they accidentally exterminate their own species…

they attacked a Foundation asset, and Murdered multiple foundation staff in cold blood-the active refusal of the Foundation to intervene should have been a red flag that the issue was considered handled, or non-threatening- regardless, ample reason for a..significant degradation of relations potentially to the point of reprisals or refusal of aid in sub-keter emergencies-.

if nothing else, id find how the GOC responded to discovering exactly WHAT they'd done in detail fascinating-wither they'd just brush the casualties/entirety of what they did in one moment of organizational knee-jerk panic off and cheerfully promise to shoot sooner/empty the magazine next time (chair incident), wither they'd make any institutional or doctrinal changes….

item.title: God Omofobich Carbon
item.content:encoded:
GOC just blowed everting….again……
thing the foundation would do only after at least 10 personell or more died

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Approving wholeheartedly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT: Timer removed, edits made.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

We have a few potential crosslinks here. First off, since the GOC plays such a major role in the article, an in line link seems appropriate. Next, we have some thematic links to SCP-1322 in terms of the disease described, and SCP-2003 with the time travel. Lastly, there's this relatively unknown tale in which this article is the main subject. Linking these pages to the article is pretty straightforward.

Change this:

On ██/██/19██, as SCP-2002 passed Earth's moon, a previously unidentified Global Occult Coalition satellite containing a high-powered carbon-dioxide laser, opened fire on SCP-2002, breaking up the main hull and dispersing the smaller spheres over a wide area.

To this:

On ██/██/19██, as SCP-2002 passed Earth's moon, a previously unidentified Global Occult Coalition satellite containing a high-powered carbon-dioxide laser, opened fire on SCP-2002, breaking up the main hull and dispersing the smaller spheres over a wide area.

Change this:

Faced with a 100% sterility rate, we found that there was a way of fertilizing embryos so they wouldn't be subject to the plague's effects.

To this:

Faced with a 100% sterility rate, we found that there was a way of fertilizing embryos so they wouldn't be subject to the plague's effects.

Change this:

Our calculations indicated a dissipation of effects approximately ███ years from day zero, so in the end we were left with no option but to send a selection of these embryos out into space, along with a crew of personnel tasked with their care.

To this:

Our calculations indicated a dissipation of effects approximately ███ years from day zero, so in the end we were left with no option but to send a selection of these embryos out into space, along with a crew of personnel tasked with their care.

Change this:

Our ETA is currently set at WARNING: temporal dislocation error detected.

To this:

Our ETA is currently set at WARNING: temporal dislocation error detected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Comment to remove deleted account votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, gives the SCP foundation more depth by showing that the foundation is still around in the future. this is great as evidence in any tales set in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wow, i think this is one of my new favorite scp, very well put together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We maintain, they change. You have a previously undetected carbon-oxide laser in orbit that is quite capable of destroying anomalies we want contained or studied. The logical course of action is to make sure no further anomalies will be destroyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing that rubs me the wrong way…

Why is the foundation blowing up a GOC satellite? Especially after the point where it's destruction could have saved everything? The two organizations have their differences, but it seems like an act of uncharacteristic aggression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how the actual existence of the SCP is more important that the SCP itself.
And also that it makes you wonder what will happen in the future, now that is being destroyed. Because not launching the rocket thing would create a paradox, right? But what would be the point of launching it if they already know what will happen to him?
Very cool! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: they didn't just assume this thing would kill everything. They had no idea if this thing was capable of slowing down and landing itself properly(only the foundation got the transmission that implied such). If it just continued at it's current speed and collided with earth it would have cause a mass-extinction event.

item.title: Re: Warning. Nerd posting.
item.content:encoded:
You figured it out. Almost broke my brains trying to get it right, but I'm glad I did. As for it getting past the censoring process, you need to remember that sometimes as an author, you need to take some in-universe liberties in order to make the puzzle or pay-off bigger for the reader beyond that fourth wall.

The fact that you figured it out only after 9 months, makes that sloppiness worth it. ;)

item.title: Warning. Nerd posting.
item.content:encoded:
Ok. So this one thing bothers me…

You mention that the object is detected at a distance of 15.8 au, heading towards Earth, which would place it somewhere past Uranus' orbit around the sun. At 12.5 kilometers per second (about 4% of the speed of light), you'd clock about 2.635 au, every year (2.642 au on a leap year).

Now, According to your classified dates, the vessel is detected at some point in the 20th century or 19XX. It's still 19XX when it passes the moon, which is only 0.0026 au from the Earth and it doesn't crash until 20XX.

This gives us a time frame of about 6 years from being detected until it reaches earth and 9 hours between passing the moon and the crash landing.

Point being, the only way for the dates to make sense is to place the events around XX/XX/1994 at the time of detection, 12/31/1999 when it passes the moon and 01/01/2000 at the time of the crash. This kinda defeats the point of classifying the dates, since it's relatively easy to figure this out, unless it was your intention to date the crash at the start of the millennium, in which case… bravo.
-slow clap-

Edit: Still, I would consider it awfully sloppy work of the SCP personnel in charge, to allow such an important piece of information get past the censoring process.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Congrats.
Yay!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, you got most of the story (you forgot to mention the part where we might have killed our own future), but there's an implication underneath it all: maybe we should stop trying to compete with other GoIs and instead start sharing information.

If we hadn't been managing a wildly succesful disinformation campaign, the GOC might have realized that, no, the thing hurtling towards earth is not a threat to existence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sooo.. SCP from the future full of unfertilized embryos made by the SCP Foundation in the future is sucked through a worm hole into the past so it was going to land waaaay before the assumed arrival date but it was shot down by a CO2 laser thing made by another organization because they thought that it would kill everything? Am I right or did I miss something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea, excellent execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't thought of the similarities, but no. For one, it would mean that there's another wormhole to that reality/universe, and the ship in question was definitely native to this reality iteration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if this spaceship came over from 1322. It would make sense, since they're supposed to have advanced technology and they've all gone sterile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, the point of the article is that they didn't… we did. We forced their hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddammit GOC why do you have to ruin everything!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No idea how I missed this comment, Clef. Sorry about that. I'll update everything tomorrow. One thing: is the reference to Operative Ames acceptable or should that be edited too?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
With the GOC canon moving to SCP Foundation, I'd appreciate it if you updated the nomenclature on this article to match.

http://www.scp-wiki.net/goc-supplemental-threat-entities

Specifically, the nomenclature should be: KTE-####-codephrase, "Typhon." Unless Typhon is the codephrase, in which you should come up with a broad definition for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, Aelanna, only saw this a few minutes ago.

Why would the Foundation not have transcribed the text from the screen capture rather than keep just the photo alone? Not only does this bother me for technical reasons due to there being no transcript in text form, but this seems like a serious breach of operational security in-universe.

Point taken. I'll add a transcription in a moment. The pic is mostly an eyecatcher, a nice little bonus to the article. I agree it makes little sense to keep just the pic around.

The term the GOC uses in the destruction of Known Threat Entities is "liquidate", not "terminate" or "neutralize".

Actually, the term 'termination' and conjugations thereof are used on the GOC Wiki when referring to non-humanoid KTEs (the slime mold for example), so I saw no problem with using them. However, 'liquidate' is also used.

item.title: It's totally okay.
item.content:encoded: Erm, yeah. Punk Faye, what Accelerando said is correct. Several successful SCP articles refer to an SCP-████, a quick example off the top of my head is SCP-1744. It's not encouraged, but definitely allowed.

item.title: Re: Is this okay or not?
item.content:encoded: I could look it up, but its not really important. Just wanted to know if it was allowed. Thanks.

item.title: Re: Is this okay or not?
item.content:encoded: I'm not sure who told you that, but I don't really see it as cross referencing. It's not blatantly trying to reference another SCP or incorporate it into the article; it's just there to show that this researcher is a figure of authority.

item.title: Is this okay or not?
item.content:encoded:
I referred to SCP-████ in a draft, and was told this is cross referencing. So you can imagine my confusion here.

This is Dr. Agnes Younts, L4 Project Lead for SCP-█████.

On topic, this was pretty good for a time warp anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the fuck, author?! :|

Thanks man, I have no idea how I managed to forget doing that1.

And thanks to FFB for helping me out. You rule.

Footnotes
1. Actually, I know exactly how I managed to forget that. The contest ended at 6 AM GMT +1, not the best time to move your article to its new home.

item.title: SCP-2000 -> SCP-2002
item.content:encoded:
I think I got it.

Crayne, if you see this you might want to scrub the article for any instances I missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has SCP-2000 instead of SCP-2002 listed a tonne of times on the page. Could someone please fix that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't read any of the rest of this discussion thread, but a couple of things I noticed while checking the tags:

  • Why would the Foundation not have transcribed the text from the screen capture rather than keep just the photo alone? Not only does this bother me for technical reasons due to there being no transcript in text form, but this seems like a serious breach of operational security in-universe.
  • The term the GOC uses in the destruction of Known Threat Entities is "liquidate", not "terminate" or "neutralize".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The crew have suffered the effects of the plague, but are no longer infectious. I'll probably clarify that once the contest is over. Good catch.

In my headcanon, the vessel got dislocated on the way back to earth. They followed the right path home, just not the right time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the GOC were perhaps-correct-by-accident, also. It's not entirely clear to me that the ship's crew are plague-free:

we found that there was a way of fertilizing embryos so they wouldn't be subject to the plague's effects. At least, not while they remained in stasis. […] we were left with no option but to send a selection of these embryos out into space, along with a crew of personnel tasked with their care. This vessel's crew and cargo will be revived and prepared for a return to earth.

This reads to me as follows:
- Embryos clear: check.
- Crew caring for embryos: unclear.
- Crew caring for embryos == vessel's [return journey] crew: unclear.

Maybe you meant to indicate that embryos, in the outer spheres, should survive, but not the crew?

One other thing: at the time of their transmission everyone was in stasis, but they were supposed to be revived for the return journey. So did they get dislocated on the way out, then? And if so, how are they heading back?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've always been a bit fond of the ways one can "cheat" in a fixed, unalterable time-line. So much so that I often forget that time travel doesn't NEED to be fixed.
Actually, given the number of times the Foundation has freaked out over a possible paradox,as well as the number of closed causal loops, it is probably true that the Foundation only wishes it lived in a universe with reliably predictable laws of time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's…a very creative approach, but you're assuming a) we're capable of sending out manned spaceships at this time and b) we know exactly how the thing worked. You're free to assume any headcanon you want of course, but in mine this isn't a feasible scenario.

The best way to approach this would be to assume that temporal causality loops can be broken and invest in a better understanding of the scip that breaches containment in 21██.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a solution to the scenario presented here, that might or might not work, but the Foundation would likely try.
Start building an exact replica now, with all evidence in place. When complete, start sending it out and back, hoping that the replica gets transported back in time. If that happened, it would be the result of a circular-causation hoax, rather than a failed plan to save humanity. Should the infertility event happen, just send out 2 or more ships, knowing one will be destroyed.
I'm tempted to say, it's "not that bad," but all options still are pretty awful and/or confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crap, I wish I'd read this earlier.

Out of curiosity, why? :)

That ending was… chilling to say the least.

Thanks. I'm glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crap, I wish I'd read this earlier.

That ending was… chilling to say the least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured a little red herring wouldn't hurt. ;)

Edit: Thanks for the upvote and the compliments by the way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, okay! That's fine, not everything has to have ~super deep references omg~. I was set to wondering because of Typhon's status as the progenitor of many monsters, and it had many humans/beings along with it, so I thought there might be some sort of darker-than-it-looks element to the whole thing (that is, that GOC - perhaps unwittingly - were actually correct to worry)

Anyway! Good luck in the contest, this is a very solid entry. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, in the drafting stages this had "Planetkiller" as a placeholder, but that was a tad blunt so I set out for a suitably ominous mythological reference. I looked at several, but I liked Typhon's sound and myth best. No deeper meaning than that I'm afraid. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you be willing to elaborate on the reasoning (if any) of your choice to have the GOC call it "Typhon"? I like the whole thing and have no critique to offer, so this is strictly a nosy-curious kind of thing (as opposed to "the reason better please me or downvote" :P)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I sure hope it came from an alternate universe. Otherwise our future's gonna be pretty shitty.

Last one to die, please turn out the lights
– hide block

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reality iteration it is. We're wise enough to at least acknowledge there's probably more realities out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like both. "Reality iteration" sounds cooler, but either one works. "Reality" is the more conservative version (conservative in the sense that it doesn't acknowledge other universes inherently).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures are a wall of text; break up that paragraph!

I honestly didn't think it was that bad, but you're right. It has been done.

Is this meant to imply our reality is constructed, in a sci-fi sort of way? Usually that phrasing is meant to imply consensus reality, not "our universe" or "our reality".

I used it mostly because it has a nice ring to it, but if it's not the right word, I'll need a replacement. Reality iteration? Or just reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures are a wall of text; break up that paragraph!

reality construct.

Is this meant to imply our reality is constructed, in a sci-fi sort of way? Usually that phrasing is meant to imply consensus reality, not "our universe" or "our reality".

Overall, not bad. Have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the reply and clarification!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of course I upvote, but could you please tell me what am I getting wrong with the spheres, like I said above, please? Thanks.

The embryos were housed in the smaller spheres. They contained life-support systems and all other systems necessary to function as cribs once the ship had safely returned to earth. Unfortunately, that didn't take into account being shot in the face with a CO2 laser. The sphere in the picture would have held 1 embryo, not 500 people.

And then you edited. Damn you.

And yes, the O5 council of 21██ did indeed clone themselves.

As for the temporal dislocation: it's a plot device intended to help the narrative along. I could elaborate, but it's unlikely we would find out exactly what went wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, yes. If I really like something on an SCP article, it's the additional warning, the additional precaution, the additional something. It's like saying - watch out, this is gonna be a wild ride! While it might be then hard to do justice to such foreshadowing of an exceptional SCP, it's always something nice to see. Be it 231, Lowell's 2000, yours or, the "Iteration" SCP also comes to mind. This also counts for the gray bar, previously unseen in SCPs. New doesn't mean bad.

Judging from the procedures, looks like Foundation really needs to conceal the info which leaks from many sources. Interesting.

A Foundation spaceship in development, meant to be in this universe, jumping back in time. Appearing as some kind of a space base. Very nice tie-in of the sci-fi theme and SCP Foundation.

The Addendum reminded me of Scrin invasion in CnC 3.

If there is a 2000 project ongoing, why expunge a possible mention in this paragraph?

under development [DATA EXPUNGED] established at that time

—(estimated to be comprised of 5 adult males, 142 newborn infant males, 21 adult females, and 377 newborn infant females)

Ew. What? I sort of not see the purpose of this being in the document. Like I said at one of the previous proposals, this is too much "Attack on Titan directed by Uwe Boll".
Did it crash into a nomad nursery? Now, if this implies that there were humans in the smaller spheres, or maybe O5 cloning grounds ( ??? but hey, could be) I sort of see the point. If so, I have no idea how 500 people would fit into the small sphere in the picture.— No, nvm, sorry.

Ok, so Foundation evacuation vessel confirmed. Cool. Babies confirmed - okey. People fitting into small spheres… not at all.

Now, I really like this proposal for the combination of the space theme and SCP Foundation issues tie-in. Even the GOC play an important role, coupled with a nicely made picture. Of course I upvote, but could you please tell me what am I getting wrong with the spheres, like I said above, please? Thanks.

All the pieces seems to fit, except for the reason of the temporal dislocation… either an accident or <headcanon> probably somebody in the future wanted to try and prevent the plague event. </headcanon>

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Not my favorite SCP-2000, but I definitely want this on the main list SOMEWHERE, and I think it's better than most. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a wacky piece of feedback: move the transmission transcript to the end of the article. It really feels like a punchline, and I think it might have a great deal more effect if we see what the fallout of the event is after the event.

You know, you're absolutely right. Will edit.

Otherwise, this is a "hey, be careful, we might have moles" ending, which isn't nearly as interesting compared to the object itself. (I think we can edit right now, yes?)

Actually, we have moles. We've had moles ever since we became a major factor in the game (something I also reference in SCP-1118). The last addendum's not supposed to be a punchline, more a nice little tying up of loose narrative ends. :)

But it's still good and I upboated. I didn't know CO2 lasers were a thing until now. Also I'd like to think that that GOC satellite was previously unknown because we never expected them to have satellites in the first place. Like a, "Wait, they're that advanced now?" kind of thing. (Also it was their only satellite and now we have it, poor gocks :()

Yeah, I found out about carbon dioxide lasers when I researched space-space weaponry. It was either that or a 23mm autocannon. And I share your assessment that it was probably their only weaponized satellite.

GOC: "Could we please have it back?"
SCPF: "No can do, we're researching it."
GOC: "It's just twisted metal now, what's the point?"
SCPF: "Look, we said no."
GOC: "But why?"
SCPF: "Just because. Fucking carbon dioxide lasers."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a wacky piece of feedback: move the transmission transcript to the end of the article. It really feels like a punchline, and I think it might have a great deal more effect if we see what the fallout of the event is after the event. Otherwise, this is a "hey, be careful, we might have moles" ending, which isn't nearly as interesting compared to the object itself. (I think we can edit right now, yes?)

But it's still good and I upboated. I didn't know CO2 lasers were a thing until now. Also I'd like to think that that GOC satellite was previously unknown because we never expected them to have satellites in the first place. Like a, "Wait, they're that advanced now?" kind of thing. (Also it was their only satellite and now we have it, poor gocks :()
